How to Organize a DeWalt ToughSystem 2.0 Drawer with 3D Printed Inserts

Published 2026-03-13 by Chris Winland

The DeWalt ToughSystem 2.0 drawer unit (DWST08320) is built like a tank but ships with zero internal organization. Tools rattle around, small bits migrate to the back, and you end up digging through a jumbled mess every time you need a 10mm socket. 3D printed custom inserts fix this permanently.

Drawer Dimensions You Need

The ToughSystem 2.0 two-drawer unit has shallow top and deep bottom drawers. Key internal measurements:

  • Top drawer: approximately 520mm x 330mm x 50mm deep
  • Bottom drawer: approximately 520mm x 330mm x 100mm deep

These are internal usable dimensions. The actual cavity is slightly larger but the drawer slides and lip reduce the printable area. Always measure your specific unit since manufacturing tolerances vary.

Why 3D Printed Inserts Beat Foam

Kaizen foam is the traditional answer for tool organization. Cut the foam to match your tools and press them in. It works, but it has real drawbacks for a drawer that opens and closes hundreds of times.

Foam compresses over time. After 6 months of daily use, the cavities loosen and tools shift. Foam also tears at thin walls between closely spaced tools. And if you change your tool loadout, you need to cut new foam.

3D printed inserts do not compress, do not tear, and can be reprinted in an afternoon if your tool loadout changes. For a ToughSystem drawer that takes real job site abuse, printed inserts are the better long-term choice.

Creating Custom Inserts with TracetoForge

The fastest way to create a precision-fit insert for your ToughSystem drawer:

  1. Lay your tools on a sheet of paper (US Letter or A4)
  2. Take a top-down photo with your phone
  3. Upload to TracetoForge
  4. Set your tray dimensions to match the drawer (custom tray mode)
  5. Trace each tool, set cavity depths individually
  6. Export STL or 3MF

For the ToughSystem 2.0, you will likely need to tile the insert across multiple prints unless you have a large-format printer like the Creality K2 Plus (350x350mm bed). TracetoForge lets you set custom tray dimensions, so you can create sections that tile together to fill the full drawer.

Layout Strategy

Top drawer (shallow): Hand tools that you grab constantly. Screwdrivers, pliers, wire strippers, utility knives. Keep cavity depth at 15-25mm so tools sit proud enough to grab quickly.

Bottom drawer (deep): Larger items. Impact driver, drill, oscillating tool, tape measures. Use deeper cavities (30-50mm) and finger notches on the heavy end of each tool so you can lift them out easily.

Group tools by task, not by type. If you always grab needle-nose pliers and wire strippers together, put them next to each other. Your insert layout should match your workflow, not your tool catalog.

Print Considerations for Large Inserts

ToughSystem drawer inserts are big prints. A full-width insert section (520mm) will not fit most printers. Split it into 2-3 tiles that sit side by side in the drawer. Design them so the tile edges align with spaces between tool cavities, not through the middle of a cavity.

Material matters here. These drawers go in work trucks. Use PETG, not PLA. See our PETG vs PLA comparison for why.

Print with 3-4 wall loops and 15-20% infill. The walls handle the structural load. Thick walls also make the insert feel solid and professional rather than flimsy.

Other ToughSystem Organization Options

DeWalt sells official ToughSystem organizer cases (like the DWST08020) with removable bins, but these are separate cases, not drawer inserts. For the drawer unit specifically, 3D printing is currently the only way to get custom tool-shaped cavities.

Some users combine approaches: Gridfinity baseplates glued into the drawer with Gridfinity bins for small parts, plus custom printed inserts for larger tools. This hybrid approach works well for drawers that hold both hand tools and consumables like screws and connectors.
